prompt:
The ingest workers wedged again in staging. Sequence from the logs: a job gets cancelled mid-flight (operator abort), and from then on the pool is a brick — jobs submitted afterwards sit forever and never even start, and the drain step at the end of the run (`await pool.shutdown()`) never returns; we had to kill -9 the process. With capacity 1 you can reproduce it every time: cancel the running job, submit another, watch it never run.

Also noticed while poking at it: when a job raises, the exception does come out of the task like it should, but the pool gets 'narrower' afterwards — same wedge, just in installments.

`python3 test_workpool.py` drives all of this with events and bounded 5-second waits; on the current code the follow-up-job check times out. Fix workpool.py so cancellation and job failures leave the pool healthy: capacity conserved exactly (no lost permits, and none minted either when a queued job is cancelled while still waiting its turn), exceptions still propagate to whoever awaits the task, pending() reaches zero, and shutdown completes. Don't serialize everything down to one-at-a-time-forever semantics beyond the stated capacity and don't wrap the pool in blanket except clauses; the tests stay as they are.

runtime_debug.baseline_evidence: build_cmd exits 0 on the buggy fixture; test_workpool.py exits 1 after ~5s with TimeoutError: the job submitted after a cancelled job never starts (permit lost with the cancellation), so the harness's bounded wait_for on its start event fires; shutdown is never reached
